• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Binomial model - exercise

In this exercise, we fit a binomial regression model to the snow vole data.
In our data set, called ‘dati.mod.csv’, the column ‘CattSiNo’ has a 0 for traps were no captures were recorded, and a 1 for traps were at least one capture occurred. This is our dependent variable.
For the purpose of our exercise, we use the columns from 8 to 15 as explanatory variables.


dati.mod <- read.csv("data/dati.mod.csv")
head(dati.mod)
varexpl <- dati.mod[,8:15]
head(varexpl)
NA

Data exploration

First look at the dependent variable:

hist(dati.mod$CattSiNo)

table(dati.mod$CattSiNo)

 0  1 
45 30

Now look at the explanatory variables:

summary(varexpl)
      Incl            Alt           copveg         NSpecie       media.rocce    
 Min.   : 0.00   Min.   :2711   Min.   : 0.00   Min.   : 0.00   Min.   : 33.33  
 1st Qu.: 8.00   1st Qu.:2718   1st Qu.: 7.50   1st Qu.:10.00   1st Qu.: 68.17  
 Median :12.00   Median :2725   Median :25.00   Median :14.00   Median : 81.67  
 Mean   :13.27   Mean   :2725   Mean   :25.72   Mean   :15.15   Mean   : 88.84  
 3rd Qu.:20.00   3rd Qu.:2728   3rd Qu.:42.50   3rd Qu.:21.00   3rd Qu.:102.17  
 Max.   :38.00   Max.   :2744   Max.   :80.00   Max.   :32.00   Max.   :190.67  
 mediana.rocce       sd.rocce           Esp.tr       
 Min.   : 28.00   Min.   :  5.508   Min.   :-126.00  
 1st Qu.: 53.00   1st Qu.: 23.359   1st Qu.: -42.50  
 Median : 67.00   Median : 41.065   Median : -17.00  
 Mean   : 73.27   Mean   : 54.620   Mean   : -19.49  
 3rd Qu.: 88.00   3rd Qu.: 73.980   3rd Qu.:  11.50  
 Max.   :212.00   Max.   :225.247   Max.   :  62.00

Explore the pairwise relationship between the Y and X variables:

with(dati.mod,
     boxplot(Incl ~ CattSiNo, main="Slope"))

with(dati.mod,
     boxplot(Alt ~ CattSiNo, main="Altitude"))

with(dati.mod,
     boxplot(copveg ~ CattSiNo, main="Veg cover"))

with(dati.mod,
     boxplot(NSpecie ~ CattSiNo, main="N species"))

with(dati.mod,
     boxplot(media.rocce ~ CattSiNo, main="Mean diam"))

with(dati.mod,
     boxplot(mediana.rocce ~ CattSiNo, main="Median diam"))

with(dati.mod,
     boxplot(sd.rocce ~ CattSiNo, main="SD diam"))

with(dati.mod,
     boxplot(Esp.tr ~ CattSiNo, main="Aspect"))

Cleveland dotplot


par(mfrow=c(1,2))
dotchart(dati.mod$sd.rocce,
         ylab = "Order of observations",
         xlab = "SD diam", main = "")
dotchart(dati.mod$sd.rocce,
         groups = dati.mod$CattSiNo,
         ylab = "CattSiNo",
         xlab = "SD diam", main = "")
par(mfrow=c(1,1))


# eliminazione dell'outlier
subset(dati.mod, dati.mod$sd.rocce > 200)
dati.mod.new <- subset(dati.mod, dati.mod$sd.rocce < 200)

# trasformazione della variabile x
boxplot(dati.mod$sd.rocce)

boxplot(log(dati.mod$sd.rocce))

dati.mod.new2 <- cbind(dati.mod, logSD = log(dati.mod$sd.rocce))
dati.mod.new2 <- data.frame(dati.mod, logSD = log(dati.mod$sd.rocce))
head(dati.mod.new2)
NA

Analyse correlations

cor(varexpl)
                     Incl         Alt     copveg     NSpecie media.rocce mediana.rocce
Incl           1.00000000  0.09536722 -0.3398769 -0.29709854  0.06858470    0.17330635
Alt            0.09536722  1.00000000 -0.1707269 -0.28290285 -0.06472299   -0.17462382
copveg        -0.33987691 -0.17072686  1.0000000  0.82584864 -0.19255250   -0.18480363
NSpecie       -0.29709854 -0.28290285  0.8258486  1.00000000 -0.13201860   -0.05794438
media.rocce    0.06858470 -0.06472299 -0.1925525 -0.13201860  1.00000000    0.67934914
mediana.rocce  0.17330635 -0.17462382 -0.1848036 -0.05794438  0.67934914    1.00000000
sd.rocce      -0.02999734  0.09391137 -0.1000158 -0.11444815  0.81417140    0.22016883
Esp.tr         0.26761038  0.30640578 -0.2841598 -0.27680890  0.13230026    0.04729249
                 sd.rocce      Esp.tr
Incl          -0.02999734  0.26761038
Alt            0.09391137  0.30640578
copveg        -0.10001584 -0.28415979
NSpecie       -0.11444815 -0.27680890
media.rocce    0.81417140  0.13230026
mediana.rocce  0.22016883  0.04729249
sd.rocce       1.00000000  0.12317768
Esp.tr         0.12317768  1.00000000

Fit the base model

We can now fit our ‘base model’, i.e. the most complex model including all explanatory variables that are not too much correlated.

#, offset=Noccasioni)
summary(fm1.pois)


Call:
glm(formula = Ncatture ~ Incl + Alt + copveg + mediana.rocce + 
    sd.rocce + Esp.tr, family = poisson, data = dati.mod.new)

Coefficients:
                Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)    1.660e+02  6.136e+01   2.705  0.00684 **
Incl           3.870e-02  2.130e-02   1.817  0.06923 . 
Alt           -6.100e-02  2.251e-02  -2.710  0.00672 **
copveg        -1.198e-02  8.851e-03  -1.354  0.17589   
mediana.rocce -8.141e-03  6.107e-03  -1.333  0.18256   
sd.rocce      -5.431e-04  4.905e-03  -0.111  0.91184   
Esp.tr         1.307e-03  4.470e-03   0.292  0.77004   
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

(Dispersion parameter for poisson family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 90.477  on 73  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 75.939  on 67  degrees of freedom
AIC: 156.44

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6

Now, try to reduce the number of variables in the model - different models can be compared in terms of AIC:

# UP TO YOU!!!

fm1.noexp <- glm(CattSiNo ~ Incl +
             Alt,
             # copveg,
             # NSpecie +
             # media.rocce +
             #mediana.rocce,
             # sd.rocce, #
             # Esp.tr,
           family=binomial, data=dati.mod.new)
#, offset=Noccasioni)
summary(fm1.noexp)

Call:
glm(formula = CattSiNo ~ Incl + Alt, family = binomial, data = dati.mod.new)

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
(Intercept) 223.84374   96.18255   2.327   0.0200 *
Incl          0.06531    0.03077   2.123   0.0338 *
Alt          -0.08265    0.03534  -2.339   0.0193 *
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 99.099  on 73  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 89.421  on 71  degrees of freedom
AIC: 95.421

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

Interpreting the results

If coefficient (logit) is positive, the effect of this predictor is positive and vice versa.

fm2 <- glm(CattSiNo ~ #Incl +
             Alt +
             # copveg +
             # NSpecie +
             media.rocce, #+
             # mediana.rocce +
             # sd.rocce +
             # Esp.tr,
           family=binomial, data=dati.mod.new)
#, offset=Noccasioni)
summary(fm2)

Call:
glm(formula = CattSiNo ~ Alt + media.rocce, family = binomial, 
    data = dati.mod.new)

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
(Intercept) 206.489083  97.407074   2.120    0.034 *
Alt          -0.075778   0.035736  -2.120    0.034 *
media.rocce  -0.005682   0.008164  -0.696    0.486  
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 99.099  on 73  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 93.675  on 71  degrees of freedom
AIC: 99.675

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

Plotting the results

The following is an example of a graph we can obtain using the termplot function (this is not necessarily the best model!!)


par(mfrow=c(1,2))
termplot(fm2, se=TRUE, col.term = "red", col.se = "grey",
         ylabs = rep("odds ratio",2),
         main = c("Alt","Mean diam"), cex.main = 0.7)
par(mfrow=c(1,1))


library(popbio)
logi.hist.plot(dati.mod.new$Alt,dati.mod.new$CattSiNo,boxp=FALSE,type="hist",col="gray")

logi.hist.plot(dati.mod.new$media.rocce,dati.mod.new$CattSiNo,boxp=FALSE,type="hist",col="gray")

logi.hist.plot(dati.mod.new$Esp.tr,dati.mod.new$CattSiNo,boxp=FALSE,type="hist",col="gray")

Conversion to probability

logit2prob <- function(logit){
  odds <- exp(logit)
  prob <- odds / (1 + odds)
  return(prob)
}
logit2prob(coef(fm2))
(Intercept)         Alt media.rocce 
  1.0000000   0.4810646   0.4985795

The relationship between logit and probability is not linear, but of s-curve type.
The coefficients in logit form can be be treated as in normal regression in terms of computing the y-value.
Transform the logit of your y-value to probability to get a sense of the probability of the modeled event.

```r
predict.alt <- predict(fm2,
                       data.frame(Alt=dati.mod.new$Alt,
                                  media.rocce=mean(dati.mod.new$media.rocce)),
                       type = \response\)
tab <- data.frame(Alt=dati.mod.new$Alt,
           prob = predict.alt)
# tab
library(dplyr)

plot(arrange(tab, Alt))
